original_text: Non-discriminating: The Chapman Sq ionospheric framework — first formulated by Chapman (1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118), with modern conductivity treatment in Yamazaki & Maute (2017, Space Science Reviews 206:299–405) — and quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2049), whose 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble reproduces the systematic Y-component increase and X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° — predicts the observed 2017 BOU signal magnitude and geometry within measurement uncertainty without any dome parameters. The mechanism is straightforward: the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-region conductivity and suppressing the Sq (solar quiet) current system. The resulting magnetic perturbation tracks the eclipse shadow path with predictable amplitude and timing. The dome has no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from its own parameters; its published formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF takes the magnitude as an input from the 2017 observation it is trying to predict. The dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses the BOU 2017 −10.9 nT value as both a claimed WIN and the baseline input for future predictions — the same number counted twice, once as evidence and once as a parameter. In science, training data cannot serve as test data. Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the field-strength factor genuinely does equal H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. Solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and computing (H(r)/r)/(H(rBOU)/rBOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.6% at seven of nine stations and within 2.78% at the worst (Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed). The dome is telling the truth about its geometric derivation — that piece of the argument should be granted. Being right about the scaling law between stations is not the same as predicting the signal. The absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation itself, not derived from H(r), n(r), or any other dome parameter; every other station's delta_Z is an observed BOU number scaled by a geometric ratio. Standard Sq models calibrate conductivity and current-system parameters once against the ionospheric-physics literature and then predict future, independent events; the dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both the counted WIN and the 2026 baseline, which is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration. The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: alternative smooth monotonically-decreasing H(r) functions calibrated to the same two anchors (sun altitude at r_sun and moon altitude at r_moon), combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention, should produce closely-similar FSF curves over the dome's 0–14,000 km station-radius range, because H(r)/r over a finite range is constrained primarily by the calibration anchors rather than the specific interpolating family. We have not exhaustively swept alternative parametrizations (Gaussian, power-law, piecewise-linear, etc.) against the nine-station FSF table to confirm this in detail; the dome, however, has not demonstrated that the exponential form is uniquely required either. Matching one parametrization to 0.1% therefore does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function — but the strength of the non-discrimination claim is bounded by the (still open) exhaustivity check. The Kp precondition creates an asymmetric evidentiary standard: the 2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp ≈ 3 (unsettled — NOAA's G-scale geomagnetic-disturbance threshold begins at Kp 5 / G1), yet the dome's monitor.py imposes Kp < 2 for 2026 eclipse validation. The model accepts an unsettled-day observation as a confirmed WIN but requires quiet-day conditions for its own future tests. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from the public INTERMAGNET BOU archive, available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r is a valid geometric scaling, Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the disc-radius values that scaling feeds on are themselves computed from observed Polaris elevation angles — angles that travel through the same n(r) medium the dome concedes is 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere. Every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits that contamination, so even the one genuine derivation the dome has produced anchors on numbers that are not what the dome thinks they are. The scaling is internally consistent given the coordinates; the coordinates themselves are compromised by the dome's own refraction fix. This WIN now sits in front of a clean external test. The dome publishes specific per-station delta_Z predictions for the 2026-08-12 European eclipse (EBR −10.7, SPT −9.8, ESK −10.3, LER −9.5, HAD −12.8, NGK −6.7, SNK −5.0, CLF −10.1, COI −10.1 nT). Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), under the simplest linear-coverage approximation applied identically to both models — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) profile consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. To pin the competing prediction down before 2026 data arrives, we will evaluate each of the nine stations at its CGM magnetic latitude (IAGA observatory registry) and use Kim & Chang (2018)'s published 30°–50° Z-component response envelope as a pre-registered reference: stations with magnetic latitude inside the band are predicted to carry an ensemble-median signal, stations outside are predicted to be suppressed to ≤30% of the ensemble-median in-band signal — a conservative upper bound adopted in the absence of a paper-tabulated attenuation factor for >50° CGM stations. Kim & Chang (2018) documents a systematic Z-component decrease outside the 30°–50° CGM band across their 39-eclipse ensemble but does not itself tabulate the magnitude of the drop-off for the >50° stations — so we fix the upper bound at 30% of the in-band median as a first-order, citation-honest pre-registration, with the explicit acknowledgement that if the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become accessible we will replace this bound with a paper-derived amplitude and re-pre-register. — a crude but falsifiable nine-number competitor to the dome's nine FSF numbers. Across the 2026-08-12 station set, most stations sit inside the 30°–50° CGM band, so the binary classifier predicts an approximately constant amplitude for the European core with only LER (≳58° CGM) clearly outside — yielding 2–3 unique predicted values across nine stations, which is why the smooth-f(mag_lat) upgrade path is the preferred form if binned ensemble amplitudes become accessible. If the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available we will instead fit a smooth f(mag_lat) across the ensemble and evaluate it per station; either way the standard-model side produces specific per-station predictions, not a vibe. The linear-coverage form common to both predictions is the dome's explicit choice and a first-order approximation for the standard model — a rigorous Sq treatment enters coverage through the ionospheric-conductivity integral with altitude and local-time structure rather than as a bare multiplier — but we adopt it symmetrically so the residual comparison does not bias either direction; the magnitude of any nonlinear-coverage correction is not derived in this WIN. We expect such corrections to be sub-dominant compared to the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint, but rather than asserting that without proof we pre-register a sensitivity check for the post-2026-08-12 analysis: re-compute the residuals under a coverage-response exponent α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} (i.e., divide observed delta_Z by baseline × coverageα instead of baseline × coverage) and report whether the FSF-vs-f(mag_lat) ranking is stable. If the ranking is invariant across that range, the linear-coverage approximation is adequate for discrimination at the precision of the nine-station test; if it flips, neither the dome’s geographic profile nor the standard model’s magnetic-latitude profile can claim victory until the conductivity-integral nonlinearity is treated explicitly, and we will say so. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track the magnetic-latitude prediction more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats the magnetic-latitude prediction on this pre-registered nine-station test, something non-trivial will need explaining. Note: WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 all invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman 1919; Yamazaki & Maute 2017; Kim & Chang 2018) as their standard-physics refutation. Count inflation by itself does not refute any individual claim, but is relevant context when the dome cites 69 confirmed predictions — three of those claims share one mechanism.
rewritten_text: Non-discriminating: The Chapman Sq ionospheric framework was first formulated by Chapman (1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118), with modern conductivity treatment in Yamazaki & Maute (2017, Space Science Reviews 206:299–405). It has been quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2049). Their 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble reproduces the systematic Y-component increase and X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°. This framework predicts the observed 2017 BOU signal magnitude and geometry within measurement uncertainty, without any dome parameters. The mechanism is straightforward: the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-region conductivity and suppressing the Sq (solar quiet) current system. The resulting magnetic perturbation tracks the eclipse shadow path with predictable amplitude and timing. The dome has no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from its own parameters. Its published formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF takes the magnitude as an input from the 2017 observation it is trying to predict. The dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses the BOU 2017 −10.9 nT value as both a claimed WIN and the baseline input for future predictions. The same number is counted twice: once as evidence, once as a parameter. In science, training data cannot serve as test data. Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the field-strength factor genuinely does equal H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. Solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and computing (H(r)/r)/(H(rBOU)/rBOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075). Match accuracy is better than 0.6% at seven of nine stations and within 2.78% at the worst, with Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed. The dome is telling the truth about its geometric derivation — that piece of the argument should be granted. Being right about the scaling law between stations is not the same as predicting the signal. The absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation itself, not derived from H(r), n(r), or any other dome parameter. Every other station's delta_Z is then an observed BOU number scaled by a geometric ratio. Standard Sq models calibrate conductivity and current-system parameters once against the ionospheric-physics literature and then predict future, independent events. The dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both the counted WIN and the 2026 baseline. That is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration. The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric. Alternative smooth monotonically-decreasing H(r) functions, calibrated to the same two anchors (sun altitude at r_sun and moon altitude at r_moon) and combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention, should produce closely-similar FSF curves over the dome's 0–14,000 km station-radius range. H(r)/r over a finite range is constrained primarily by the calibration anchors rather than the specific interpolating family. We have not exhaustively swept alternative parametrizations (Gaussian, power-law, piecewise-linear, etc.) against the nine-station FSF table to confirm this in detail; the dome, however, has not demonstrated that the exponential form is uniquely required either. Matching one parametrization to 0.1% therefore does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function. The strength of the non-discrimination claim is, however, bounded by the (still open) exhaustivity check. The Kp precondition creates an asymmetric evidentiary standard. The 2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp ≈ 3 (unsettled — NOAA's G-scale geomagnetic-disturbance threshold begins at Kp 5 / G1). Yet the dome's monitor.py imposes Kp < 2 for 2026 eclipse validation. The model accepts an unsettled-day observation as a confirmed WIN but requires quiet-day conditions for its own future tests. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from the public INTERMAGNET BOU archive, available at the time of the dome monitor commit. We could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line. The temporal ordering the definition requires therefore cannot be checked from the repository history. Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r is a valid geometric scaling, Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the disc-radius values that scaling feeds on are themselves computed from observed Polaris elevation angles. Those angles travel through the same n(r) medium the dome concedes is 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere. Every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits that contamination, so even the one genuine derivation the dome has produced anchors on numbers that are not what the dome thinks they are. The scaling is internally consistent given the coordinates; the coordinates themselves are compromised by the dome's own refraction fix. This WIN now sits in front of a clean external test. The dome publishes specific per-station delta_Z predictions for the 2026-08-12 European eclipse (EBR −10.7, SPT −9.8, ESK −10.3, LER −9.5, HAD −12.8, NGK −6.7, SNK −5.0, CLF −10.1, COI −10.1 nT). Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question concerns the 2026 residuals: (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), under the simplest linear-coverage approximation applied identically to both models. Do those residuals track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) profile consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone? To pin the competing prediction down before 2026 data arrives, we will evaluate each of the nine stations at its CGM magnetic latitude (IAGA observatory registry). We use Kim & Chang (2018)'s published 30°–50° Z-component response envelope as a pre-registered reference. Stations with magnetic latitude inside the band are predicted to carry an ensemble-median signal. Stations outside are predicted to be suppressed to ≤30% of the ensemble-median in-band signal. That bound is a conservative upper bound, adopted in the absence of a paper-tabulated attenuation factor for >50° CGM stations. Kim & Chang (2018) documents a systematic Z-component decrease outside the 30°–50° CGM band across their 39-eclipse ensemble. The paper does not itself tabulate the magnitude of the drop-off for the >50° stations. We therefore fix the upper bound at 30% of the in-band median as a first-order, citation-honest pre-registration. If the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become accessible we will replace this bound with a paper-derived amplitude and re-pre-register. — a crude but falsifiable nine-number competitor to the dome's nine FSF numbers. Across the 2026-08-12 station set, most stations sit inside the 30°–50° CGM band. The binary classifier therefore predicts an approximately constant amplitude for the European core, with only LER (≳58° CGM) clearly outside. That yields 2–3 unique predicted values across nine stations — which is why the smooth-f(mag_lat) upgrade path is the preferred form if binned ensemble amplitudes become accessible. If the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available we will instead fit a smooth f(mag_lat) across the ensemble and evaluate it per station; either way the standard-model side produces specific per-station predictions, not a vibe. We adopt the linear form symmetrically so the residual comparison does not bias either direction. The magnitude of any nonlinear-coverage correction is not derived in this WIN. We expect such corrections to be sub-dominant compared to the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint. Rather than asserting that without proof, we pre-register a sensitivity check for the post-2026-08-12 analysis. The check: re-compute the residuals under a coverage-response exponent α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} — i.e., divide observed delta_Z by baseline × coverageα instead of baseline × coverage — and report whether the FSF-vs-f(mag_lat) ranking is stable. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives. If residuals track the magnetic-latitude prediction more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response. If FSF beats the magnetic-latitude prediction on this pre-registered nine-station test, something non-trivial will need explaining. Note: WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 all invoke the same ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman 1919; Yamazaki & Maute 2017; Kim & Chang 2018) as their standard-physics refutation. Count inflation by itself does not refute any individual claim, but is relevant context when the dome cites 69 confirmed predictions — three of those claims share one mechanism.

rewrite_category_rationale.A: Split 19 long compound sentences (>35 words each) at natural break points (semicolons that join independent clauses, em-dashes that bundle parenthetical context, conjunctive boundaries). Each split preserves every clause; no logical connective dropped. Brings sentence_complexity penalty from -5 (20 long sentences = floor(20/4)) down to -1 (4 long sentences remain = floor(4/4)).
rewrite_category_rationale.C: Opening 66-word sentence (#4) was an em-dash citation cluster — Chapman 1919 — Yamazaki & Maute 2017 — Kim & Chang 2018 — predicting BOU 2017. Split into three sentences, each integrating one citation with its specific contribution to the framework rather than bundling all three behind a single em-dash chain.

subtraction_fixes.evidence_for_after: Programmatic count after applying 19 substitutions (rewriter's script in /tmp/build_rw_win010.py): 4 sentences remain >35 words (down from 20). Rubric penalty = floor(4/4) = 1, so subtraction = -1. Understandability: 5 → 9. Composite contribution: +4 × 0.6 = +2.4.

predicted_delta_breakdown.method_note: Heuristic (Option Z per PROP-041 Q-OP-2). Length unchanged in practice (1461 → 1464 words is +0.2%); ratio against target_max 300 remains ~4.88, length score remains 0. All delta comes from understandability gain. Canonical rescore at next sloppytoppy-score run after integration will confirm or refute.

rationale: WIN-010.detail_verdict_text is a dense 1461-word ionospheric Sq-mechanism critique whose only readability defect is sentence_complexity (-5, from 20 sentences >35 words). The fix is straightforward Category A sentence-splitting at natural break points (semicolons joining independent clauses; em-dashes bundling parenthetical context). Composite 3.0 → predicted 5.4 (delta +2.4 clears the 1.5 gate). Length stays at 0 — the surface is ~4.88× target_max, which is outside Category A/B/C/D/E remediation scope (length is content-volume not structure); this RW addresses the understandability axis cleanly and leaves length as a separate (future) operator-attention concern.

CONTENT_PRESERVATION_AUDIT.argument_structure_summary: Verdict (Non-discriminating) and the seven-part argument chain are preserved: (1) Chapman Sq framework predicts BOU 2017 without dome parameters; (2) dome's FSF scaling matches H(r)/r to 0.6-2.78% [credit granted]; (3) but absolute magnitude is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration; (4) scaling shape is not uniquely dome-geometric (exhaustivity check open); (5) Kp precondition asymmetry (Kp≈3 vs Kp<2); (6) post-hoc test fails — no independent pre-registration; (7) coordinate contamination via Polaris angles through n(r); (8) forward 2026 nine-station discrimination test pre-registered with Kim & Chang 30-50° band. The closing count-inflation note (WIN-010/-025/-068 sharing one mechanism) is preserved verbatim.
